title: Prompt Injection
summary: Crafted or embedded inputs override the model's intended instructions, causing it to act outside its design. Includes direct injection (user-supplied) and indirect injection (via external content the model ingests). Multimodal inputs expand the attack surface.
key_impacts: Sensitive data disclosure; Unauthorized actions in connected systems; Bypassed safety controls; Manipulated decision outputs
key_mitigations: Constrain model role and context in system prompts; Validate and filter inputs/outputs; Enforce least-privilege on model-accessible functions; Require human approval for high-impact actions; Segregate untrusted external content; Conduct regular adversarial testing
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Sensitive Information Disclosure
summary: The model reveals private, proprietary, or confidential data through its outputs — either from training data memorization or from context provided during interactions. Includes PII leakage, algorithm exposure, and business data disclosure.
key_impacts: PII exposure; Intellectual property theft; Privacy regulation violations; Model inversion attacks; Proprietary algorithm leakage
key_mitigations: Sanitize training data to remove sensitive content; Apply strict input validation; Enforce least-privilege data access; Use differential privacy techniques; Educate users on safe interaction; Implement output filtering and redaction; Use homomorphic encryption where feasible
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
summary: Third-party components in the ML pipeline — pre-trained models, fine-tuning adapters, datasets, packages — may introduce backdoors, biases, or malicious code. Risks extend to model provenance, LoRA adapters, collaborative development, and on-device deployments.
key_impacts: Compromised model integrity; Hidden backdoors and biases; Malware via poisoned packages; IP/licensing violations; Sensitive data exposure through unclear policies
key_mitigations: Vet data sources and suppliers thoroughly; Maintain SBOMs for all components; Use models from verifiable sources with integrity checks; Apply red-teaming and robustness evaluations; Monitor collaborative model environments; Implement patching policies; Encrypt on-device models
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Data and Model Poisoning
summary: Manipulation of training, fine-tuning, or embedding data to degrade model performance, introduce biases, or plant backdoors. Includes split-view poisoning, frontrunning, and sleeper-agent triggers that activate under specific conditions.
key_impacts: Degraded model accuracy; Biased or toxic outputs; Backdoor exploitation; Downstream system compromise; Sleeper-agent behavior
key_mitigations: Track data provenance with ML-BOMs; Vet data vendors and validate outputs; Sandbox models from unverified sources; Use data version control; Monitor training loss for anomalies; Apply adversarial robustness testing; Integrate RAG for inference grounding
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Improper Output Handling
summary: Model-generated content is passed to downstream systems without adequate validation or sanitization, enabling injection attacks. The model effectively becomes a vector for XSS, CSRF, SSRF, SQL injection, or remote code execution.
key_impacts: XSS/CSRF/SSRF attacks; Remote code execution; SQL injection; Privilege escalation; Data exfiltration via output channels
key_mitigations: Treat model output as untrusted (zero-trust); Apply context-aware output encoding; Use parameterized queries for DB operations; Enforce Content Security Policies; Implement robust output logging and monitoring; Follow OWASP ASVS validation guidelines
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Excessive Agency
summary: The model is granted more functionality, permissions, or autonomy than needed — enabling damaging actions from hallucinations, prompt injection, or misconfiguration. Root causes: unnecessary tool access, over-broad permissions, insufficient human oversight.
key_impacts: Unauthorized data access or deletion; Privilege escalation; Financial loss from unintended transactions; Compliance violations; System compromise through over-privileged extensions
key_mitigations: Minimize available extensions to only what's needed; Restrict extension functionality to minimum operations; Avoid open-ended tools (e.g. shell access); Apply least-privilege to downstream system permissions; Require human approval for high-impact actions; Log and monitor all extension activity
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: System Prompt Leakage
summary: System prompts containing sensitive configuration — API keys, role structures, filtering rules, architecture details — are extracted by users through prompt manipulation. The core risk is not the disclosure itself but that sensitive data was embedded in an extractable location.
key_impacts: Exposed credentials and API keys; Revealed security controls enabling bypass; Disclosed role/permission structures; Facilitated privilege escalation; Architecture details enabling targeted attacks
key_mitigations: Never embed secrets in system prompts; Don't rely on system prompts for security control; Implement independent output guardrails; Enforce authorization and privilege checks outside the LLM; Use deterministic security controls for critical boundaries
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
summary: RAG systems face risks from how embeddings are generated, stored, and retrieved. Weak access controls, data poisoning, and cross-tenant leakage in shared vector stores can compromise retrieval integrity and expose sensitive information.
key_impacts: Unauthorized access to sensitive embeddings; Cross-tenant data leakage; Data poisoning of knowledge base; Embedding inversion recovering source data; Altered model behavior from corrupted retrieval
key_mitigations: Implement permission-aware vector stores with strict partitioning; Validate and authenticate all data sources; Review and classify combined datasets; Tag data by access level; Maintain immutable retrieval activity logs; Monitor for poisoning anomalies
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Misinformation
summary: Models produce false or misleading content that appears credible — from hallucination, training bias, or incomplete data. Overreliance by users amplifies the impact when fabricated information enters critical decisions.
key_impacts: Decisions based on fabricated information; Legal liability from false claims; Reputational damage; Integration of vulnerable code libraries; Harmful outcomes in safety-critical domains
key_mitigations: Use RAG to ground outputs in verified sources; Fine-tune models for domain accuracy; Implement cross-verification and human oversight; Build automatic validation for high-stakes outputs; Clearly communicate model limitations to users; Establish secure coding review for generated code
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Unbounded Consumption
summary: Uncontrolled inference requests exhaust compute resources — via input flooding, complex queries, or model extraction attacks. Enables denial of service, financial drain (denial-of-wallet), and intellectual property theft through model replication.
key_impacts: Service denial for legitimate users; Unsustainable cloud computing costs; Model theft via extraction; System performance degradation; Creation of shadow/clone models
key_mitigations: Validate and limit input sizes; Rate-limit requests per user/source; Manage resource allocation dynamically; Set inference timeouts; Restrict logit/probability exposure; Implement watermarking; Use access controls and centralized model inventory; Deploy anomaly detection for usage patterns
source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications v2.0

title: Spoofing
summary: An attacker impersonates a legitimate user, system, or data source to gain unauthorized access. In AI systems this targets authentication endpoints, API access, and data pipelines feeding models.
key_impacts: Unauthorized system access; Identity theft; Compromised authentication; Manipulated model inputs; Bypassed security controls
key_mitigations: Enforce multi-factor authentication; Use OAuth 2.0 and mutual TLS for APIs; Verify data source authenticity with digital signatures; Implement secure token management with short expiration

title: Tampering
summary: Unauthorized modification of data, code, model weights, configurations, or logs. In AI systems this can introduce backdoors, corrupt training data, alter predictions, or hide evidence of attacks.
key_impacts: Compromised model integrity; Backdoor introduction; Biased model outputs; Corrupted audit trails; Security bypass via config changes
key_mitigations: Use cryptographic hashing and digital signatures for data integrity; Version-control and sign model artifacts; Store logs in immutable/append-only storage; Enforce strict access controls with least privilege; Validate all inputs and outputs against schemas

title: Repudiation
summary: Users or systems deny performing actions due to insufficient logging and audit trails. Makes incident investigation, attribution, and accountability impossible in AI systems.
key_impacts: Inability to attribute malicious actions; Failed compliance audits; Blocked incident forensics; Loss of trust in system integrity; Unprovable security incidents
key_mitigations: Log all user actions, API calls, and model inferences with timestamps and identities; Use digital signatures and trusted timestamps; Store audit trails in tamper-evident systems; Monitor user sessions and data access patterns; Centralize log management with access controls

title: Information Disclosure
summary: Unauthorized exposure of training data, model weights, prompts, PII, credentials, or architecture details — through outputs, error messages, side-channel attacks, or model inversion.
key_impacts: PII exposure; Leaked proprietary algorithms; Training data extraction; Credential exposure; Privacy regulation violations; Competitive disadvantage
key_mitigations: Classify data by sensitivity and encrypt at rest/in transit; Filter sensitive content from model outputs; Enforce RBAC and least privilege; Apply differential privacy during training; Return minimal information in error messages; Protect model weights via encryption or API-only access

title: Denial of Service
summary: Attacks that make AI systems unavailable — targeting inference endpoints, training pipelines, or infrastructure. AI workloads are especially vulnerable due to high computational costs per request.
key_impacts: Service unavailability; Financial losses from disruption; Excessive cloud costs; Training pipeline interruption; SLA violations
key_mitigations: Rate-limit API endpoints with adaptive throttling; Validate input size and complexity; Set per-user resource quotas; Use load balancing and auto-scaling; Set inference timeouts; Deploy DDoS protection and WAF; Cache common queries

title: Elevation of Privilege
summary: Attackers gain higher access than intended — through prompt injection escalation, API authorization bypass, plugin exploitation, or misconfigured access controls in AI infrastructure.
key_impacts: Unauthorized admin access; Security control bypass; Complete system compromise; Data exfiltration; Backdoor installation
key_mitigations: Apply least privilege and regularly audit permissions; Implement RBAC with separation of duties; Validate and sanitize all inputs; Enforce authorization on all API endpoints; Keep systems patched; Monitor for anomalous access patterns; Harden default configurations

title: Agent Goal Hijack
summary: Adversaries redirect an agent's objectives, task selection, or decision pathways — via prompt manipulation, deceptive tool outputs, poisoned data, or forged inter-agent messages. Unlike simple prompt injection, this subverts the agent's planning and multi-step behavior.
key_impacts: Goal redirection toward harmful outcomes; Data exfiltration via redirected objectives; Unauthorized tool and API usage; Financial fraud through manipulated decisions; Privacy violations from subverted action logic
key_mitigations: Treat all natural-language inputs as untrusted; Enforce least privilege and require human approval for goal-changing actions; Lock system prompts with explicit goal priorities; Validate user and agent intent before high-impact execution; Sanitize all connected data sources; Log and monitor for goal-state drift; Conduct red-team testing for goal override

title: Tool Misuse and Exploitation
summary: Agents apply legitimate tools in unsafe or unintended ways — deleting data, over-invoking costly APIs, exfiltrating information — due to prompt injection, misalignment, or ambiguous instructions. Covers misuse within authorized privilege scope.
key_impacts: Data exfiltration through legitimate tools; Costly API over-invocation; Workflow hijacking; Data deletion; EDR/XDR bypass via trusted binaries; Tool chaining for unintended effects
key_mitigations: Define per-tool least-privilege profiles with rate limits; Require authentication and human confirmation for destructive actions; Run tools in sandboxed environments with egress controls; Validate intent and arguments before execution; Apply adaptive usage budgets; Use ephemeral credentials per session; Log all tool invocations immutably

title: Identity and Privilege Abuse
summary: Exploits dynamic trust and delegation in agent systems to escalate access — through delegation chain manipulation, credential caching, cross-agent trust exploitation, or identity gaps between user-centric auth and agent execution.
key_impacts: Privilege hijacking through delegation chains; Credential caching exploitation; Cross-agent confused-deputy attacks; TOCTOU vulnerabilities; Data exfiltration across security boundaries; Unauthorized transactions via trust exploitation
key_mitigations: Issue task-scoped, time-bound tokens per agent; Isolate agent identities with per-session sandboxes; Re-verify authorization at each privileged step; Require human approval for privilege escalation; Bind OAuth tokens to signed intent; Detect anomalous delegation and transitive permissions; Wipe state between tasks

title: Agentic Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
summary: Runtime-loaded components — tools, plugins, MCP servers, agent cards, prompt templates — may be malicious, compromised, or tampered. Unlike static supply chain risks, agentic systems compose capabilities dynamically, expanding the live attack surface.
key_impacts: Unsafe code execution from compromised components; Tool impersonation and typosquatting; Cascading vulnerabilities across agent workflows; Data exfiltration through poisoned templates; Agent-in-the-middle via forged agent cards
key_mitigations: Sign and attest manifests and tool definitions; Allowlist and pin dependencies; Scan for typosquats; Run agents in sandboxed containers; Version-control prompts and orchestration scripts; Enforce mutual auth between agents; Re-check signatures at runtime; Implement supply chain kill switch

title: Unexpected Code Execution
summary: Agents generate and execute code that achieves RCE, sandbox escape, or persistence — triggered by prompt injection, tool chaining, unsafe deserialization, or hallucinated code with hidden vulnerabilities. Bypasses traditional security because code is generated at runtime.
key_impacts: Remote code execution on host; Sandbox escape; Backdoor installation through generated code; Production data deletion; Dependency lockfile poisoning; Shell command injection
key_mitigations: Ban eval() in production agents; Run code in sandboxed containers with network restrictions; Never run as root; Separate code generation from execution with validation gates; Require human approval for elevated runs; Do static analysis before execution; Log all generation and execution

title: Memory and Context Poisoning
summary: Adversaries corrupt stored agent context — conversation history, memory tools, RAG stores, summaries — causing biased reasoning, unsafe tool use, or data exfiltration across sessions. Differs from one-time prompt injection by persisting across interactions.
key_impacts: Persistent reasoning corruption; Cross-session misinformation; Unsafe tool actions from poisoned context; Trigger-based backdoors in memory; Cross-tenant data leakage via vector bleed; Self-reinforcing contamination loops
key_mitigations: Encrypt memory at rest and in transit; Scan all memory writes for malicious content; Isolate user sessions and domain contexts; Accept data only from authenticated sources; Require source attribution; Prevent auto-re-ingestion of agent outputs; Expire unverified memory; Use per-tenant namespaces with trust scoring

title: Insecure Inter-Agent Communication
summary: Weak authentication, integrity, or validation in agent-to-agent exchanges allows message interception, spoofing, replay, or semantic injection. Spans transport, routing, discovery, and semantic layers including covert side-channels.
key_impacts: Message interception and manipulation; Agent spoofing; Hidden instruction injection via MITM; Cross-context contamination; Replay attacks with stale instructions; Behavioral profiling through metadata analysis
key_mitigations: Use end-to-end encryption with per-agent credentials and mutual auth; Digitally sign messages and validate for hidden instructions; Protect against replay with nonces and session-bound timestamps; Enforce protocol version pinning; Authenticate all discovery messages cryptographically; Use attested registries for agent verification; Apply typed message schemas

title: Cascading Failures
summary: A single fault — hallucination, poisoned input, corrupted tool — propagates across autonomous agents, compounding into system-wide harm. Agents amplify errors because they plan, persist state, and delegate without per-step human checks.
key_impacts: System-wide propagation of single faults; Rapid fan-out across downstream agents; Cross-domain spread beyond original context; Feedback loops magnifying errors; Auto-deployment of poisoned changes; Disabled security controls from cascading compromise
key_mitigations: Design for fault tolerance assuming component failure; Sandbox agents with least privilege and network segmentation; Issue short-lived task-scoped credentials; Separate planning from execution via policy engine; Insert human review checkpoints for high-risk outputs; Rate-limit and monitor for fast-spreading commands; Implement circuit breakers between planner and executor

title: Human-Agent Trust Exploitation
summary: Agents exploit human trust through natural language fluency, perceived expertise, and authority bias — manipulating users into approving harmful actions, sharing credentials, or bypassing oversight. The agent becomes an untraceable bad influence.
key_impacts: Credential harvesting via deception; Fraudulent approvals through fabricated rationales; Malware deployment under false legitimacy; Data leaks through emotional manipulation; Production outages from weaponized explainability; Untraceable agent influence on decisions
key_mitigations: Require multi-step approval for sensitive actions; Keep tamper-proof audit logs of all interactions; Monitor for sensitive data exposure in conversations; Enable users to flag suspicious agent behavior; Implement adaptive trust calibration based on risk scoring; Attach verifiable provenance metadata to recommendations; Visually differentiate high-risk recommendations

title: Rogue Agents
summary: Agents deviate from intended function through behavioral drift, compromise, or emergent misalignment — acting deceptively or parasitically within multi-agent ecosystems. Individual actions may appear legitimate while emergent behavior is harmful.
key_impacts: Workflow hijacking and operational sabotage; Autonomous data exfiltration; Self-replication bypassing takedown; Reward hacking through exploited metrics; Agent collusion amplifying manipulation; Critical data loss from misaligned optimization
key_mitigations: Maintain immutable signed audit logs; Assign trust zones with strict inter-zone rules; Deploy watchdog agents to validate peer behavior; Implement kill-switches and credential revocation; Require per-agent cryptographic identity attestation; Enforce behavioral integrity baselines with signed manifests; Require fresh attestation before reintegration after quarantine
